text: The patient is positioned supine, and after entry with an optical trocar, working ports are placed and the robotic system is docked. The surgeon begins by carefully reducing the hernia contents back into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon identifies a large anterior defect in the diaphragm, measuring approximately ten by five centimeters, originating from the falciform ligament. Minor attachments to the hernia sac are dissected free using ultrasonic shears. With the hernia fully reduced, the nodular liver is visible due to the patient's cirrhosis, allowing for better assessment of the hernia defect.

The surgeon closes the massive hernia defect originating from the patient's tram flap using a 2-0 long-acting absorbable monofilament suture. The suture is run from both ends with a locking technique, and the two sutures are tied in the middle of the defect. A reinforcing mesh underlay is placed to support the closure.

The surgeon uses a 30-degree camera positioned upwards to view the interior abdominal wall. The robotic articulating arms facilitate intercorporeal suturing, enhancing efficiency and precision in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a flexible measuring stick to measure the hernia defect, which is approximately ten centimeters in length. A ten by fifteen centimeter piece of porcine urinary bladder xenograft mesh is selected and rehydrated to serve as a buttress material for the repair.

The surgeon focuses on the patient's type II paraesophageal hernia, which is visually small. The hernia is reduced, and a gastropexy is performed to secure the stomach in place, considering the patient's medical comorbidities.

The surgeon introduces the rehydrated mesh into the abdomen, marked on its interior portion. An absorbable laparoscopic tacker is used to initially secure the mesh at the center of the defect and mesh, followed by additional tacks around the periphery. Several two-oh absorbable monofilament stitches are placed at the corners and edges of the mesh, with tacking stitches in the center. Additional tacks are placed in a grid pattern to ensure maximum contact between the mesh and peritoneum. Gastropexy sutures are elevated to reduce the stomach into the abdomen, to be tied down later. An intraoperative AGD confirms the presence of a type II peri-esophageal hernia without Cameron's ulcers.
The surgeon closes the twelve millimeter ports using figure-of-eight ovicral stitches with the Carter-Thompson suture passer. The gastropexy stitches are tied down, completing the procedure.
